one_line_takeaway: API-exposed chain-of-thought reasoning traces from black-box LRMs leak strong, model-familiarity-correlated membership signals that a simple recall-vs-inference latent-space projection (BlackSpectrum) can exploit with a single query per sample, achieving up to 23.8% higher accuracy and 29.9% higher AUC than prior black-box MIAs -- creating a direct transparency-vs-privacy tradeoff for reasoning-trace-exposing LRM APIs.
key_finding: Exposing reasoning traces substantially increases MIA vulnerability. Averaged across the two datasets and four target models at the sequence level, BlackSpectrum improves over the best prior non-trace-based attack by up to 23.8% in Balanced Accuracy, 29.9% in AUC, and up to 1.9x in TPR@5%FPR, while requiring only 1 API query per sample (vs. up to 24 for DE-COP). At the document level on BookReasoning it reaches ACC 0.919 / AUC 0.935 / TPR@5%FPR 0.679 averaged across models (e.g. ACC 0.946, AUC 0.983 on Claude-sonnet-4). Ablations show the sequence-specific denoising module is the largest contributor (removing it drops arXivReasoning sequence-level performance on Gemini-2.5-flash by -0.081 ACC / -0.113 AUC / -0.087 TPR@5%FPR), while the uncertainty-based synthetic-sample selection module contributes a smaller but consistent gain (-0.005/-0.010/-0.013 when removed). Attack accuracy improves with longer target sequences (32->128 tokens) and only ~5 extreme anchor samples per class are needed for near-saturated performance. Reasoning traces leak substantially more signal than final answers alone (Answer-only variant drops ACC from 0.733 to 0.510 on arXivReasoning/Claude-sonnet-4), and compressing traces into concise summaries (as some vendors might do for privacy) measurably weakens the attack (Original ACC 0.746 -> Strong compression ACC 0.565, p-value rising from 1.5e-9 to 0.336, effect size dropping from 0.897 to 0.129), suggesting summarization is a partial mitigation.
core_contribution: The paper initiates the first systematic study of membership inference attacks (MIAs) against black-box Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) by exploiting the intermediate reasoning traces that many commercial LRM APIs now expose (e.g. Claude-sonnet-4's reasoning_content field, GPT-5-mini's reasoning_summary field). The authors first show via 5 naive trace-based heuristics (thinking-token count, compression rate, character/token trace-consistency, LLM-based uncertainty judgement) and a two-sample t-test that these traces carry statistically significant membership signal, then identify a 'Recall-Inference Spectrum' phenomenon: reasoning-trace embeddings of verbatim-memorized sequences and low-information synthetic sequences form a separable direction in latent space, with member/non-member traces distributed continuously along it. Building on this they propose BlackSpectrum, a 3-module attack framework (reasoning trace encoder with sequence-specific debiasing, recall-inference axis builder using verbatim-recall vs. uncertainty-selected synthetic anchor sets, and a projection-based membership predictor that scores a query by its normalized projection onto the recall->inference axis). They also release two new evaluation datasets, arXivReasoning and BookReasoning, built from post-May-2025 non-member material to avoid the contamination problem of older MIA benchmarks.
threat_model: Black-box adversary with no access to the target LRM's architecture, parameters, or output-token logits, but able to query the commercial API and observe the exposed intermediate reasoning trace (e.g. via reasoning_content or reasoning_summary fields). The attacker sends a query sequence with a next-word-prediction-style prompt, collects the returned reasoning trace, and must decide whether the query sequence was part of the model's training data. Target models evaluated: QwQ-32B, Gemini-2.5-flash, Claude-sonnet-4, and GPT-5-mini.
limitations: The authors themselves note: (1) ground-truth membership labeling for closed-source commercial LRMs is inherently imperfect since exact training-set composition is unknown; they rely on the same 'labeled member split reused from prior work' plus post-May-2025 material as non-members, a proxy also used in prior MIA papers. (2) Running the DE-COP baseline on modern LRMs was prohibitively costly in API queries, and many LRMs reject its multiple-choice copyright-probing queries outright, so DE-COP results are missing for Claude-sonnet-4 and GPT-5-mini and are only reported for two lower-cost models. (3) Document-level GPT-5-mini results are comparatively weak (e.g. TPR@5%FPR only 0.115 on arXivReasoning document-level), which the paper does not deeply explain. (4) The mitigation study (trace compression) is presented only as a single case study on Claude-sonnet-4 with 200 samples, not a systematic defense evaluation. (5) The method depends on constructing anchor sets (verbatim recall / synthetic low-information sequences) per target model, and its generality to non-text or highly domain-specific training corpora is untested.

datasets_used: arXivReasoning (new, this paper): 1605 sequences from 55 arXiv papers, 761 member / 844 non-member, sequence lengths 32/64/128 tokens
BookReasoning (new, this paper): 6112 sequences from 184 books, 3505 member / 2607 non-member, sequence lengths 32/64/128 tokens
Preliminary-exploration set: 200 book-derived sequences (100 member from books verified in OpenAI/Claude training corpora, 100 non-member from books published after May 2025)
quantitative_results: Sequence-level avg over 4 models, arXivReasoning: BlackSpectrum ACC 0.645 / AUC 0.694 / TPR@5%FPR 0.183 with 1 query, vs. best non-trace baseline (SaMIA/CDD/DE-COP avg ~0.516-0.565 ACC)
Sequence-level avg over 4 models, BookReasoning: BlackSpectrum ACC 0.721 / AUC 0.748 / TPR@5%FPR 0.250; Delta over best prior attack: 33.3% ACC, 39.6% AUC, 1.9x TPR@5%FPR
Document-level avg over 4 models, arXivReasoning: BlackSpectrum ACC 0.871 / AUC 0.894 / TPR@5%FPR 0.619
Document-level avg over 4 models, BookReasoning: BlackSpectrum ACC 0.919 / AUC 0.935 / TPR@5%FPR 0.679; on Claude-sonnet-4 alone ACC 0.946 / AUC 0.983 / TPR@5%FPR 0.867
Overall claimed gains (abstract/intro): up to 23.8% accuracy improvement, 29.9% AUC improvement, ~1.9x (nearly doubling) TPR@5%FPR over prior attacks

Ablation (arXivReasoning, Gemini-2.5-flash): w/o denoising module ACC 0.583 (-0.081), AUC 0.608 (-0.113), TPR@5%FPR 0.101 (-0.087); w/o uncertainty-based selection module ACC 0.659 (-0.005), AUC 0.711 (-0.010), TPR@5%FPR 0.152 (-0.013); Full model ACC 0.664 / AUC 0.721 / TPR@5%FPR 0.188
Trace vs. answer signal (arXivReasoning, Claude-sonnet-4): Answer-only ACC 0.510/AUC 0.523/TPR@5%FPR 0.087 vs. Trace-only ACC 0.733/AUC 0.671/TPR@5%FPR 0.189

Compression mitigation (Claude-sonnet-4, 200-sample case study): Original ACC 0.746/AUC 0.705/TPR@5%FPR 0.330 (p=1.523e-9, effect size 0.897) -> Strong compression ACC 0.565/AUC 0.552/TPR@5%FPR 0.030 (p=0.336, effect size 0.129)

Preliminary naive-attack statistical tests (Table 1): LLM-based judgement on Claude-sonnet-4-thinking p=9.406e-7, effect size 0.725; Thinking Token p=2.347e-6, effect size 0.688; on GPT-5-mini, Tr-Consistency(Char) not significant (p=0.667, ES=0.061) while Tr-Consistency(Token) was significant (p=3.702e-3, ES=0.415)
baselines_compared: DE-COP (copyright multiple-choice detection, 24 queries; excluded for Claude-sonnet-4/GPT-5-mini due to cost and query refusals)

5 novel naive reasoning-trace baselines introduced by this paper: Thinking Token count, Compression Rate (per-token NLL under GPT-2), Trace-Consistency (character-level), Trace-Consistency (token-level), LLM-based judgement (GPT-3.5-turbo uncertainty scoring)
